content.text: Responsive design is the practice of building web interfaces that work well at any viewport size — from a 320px phone to a 2560px monitor. Rather than building separate sites for different devices, a single HTML and CSS codebase adapts its layout, typography, and imagery through fluid units, media queries, and modern container queries.

attrs.alt: Responsive design overview: mobile-first cascade, breakpoints, fluid vs fixed, container queries, and viewport/image handling

content.text: Without this tag, mobile browsers render pages at a fake desktop width (typically 980px) and scale down. The viewport meta tag tells the browser to use the actual device width:
sets the viewport width to the device's physical width in CSS pixels.
includes this by default in every generated project.
. Disabling pinch-zoom breaks accessibility for users with low vision — it fails WCAG 1.4.4.
Mobile-first means writing base styles that target small screens, then using
media queries to progressively enhance larger viewports. This approach has two advantages: smaller devices load less CSS, and the cascade naturally builds from simple to complex.
queries — often results in more overrides and harder-to-read cascade. Mobile-first is the industry standard for new projects.
Media queries can test viewport dimensions, orientation, pointer precision, color scheme preference, and more:
There is no universally correct set of breakpoints — they should be defined by your content, not by device sizes. That said, most design systems converge on a similar set:
Define breakpoints as CSS custom properties or SCSS variables so they stay in sync across the codebase:
creates values that scale smoothly between breakpoints without any media query:

preferred value ensures the font scales with the viewport while the rem offset prevents it from collapsing too small on narrow screens. This is the modern replacement for stepped media-query typography.

— enabling the same component to adapt based on where it is placed, not the screen size. This makes components truly context-aware and reusable.

Container queries are supported in all modern browsers since Chrome 105 / Firefox 110 / Safari 16 (2022–2023). They are safe to use in new Angular projects targeting modern browsers.
Images need special handling for responsiveness — serving a 2000px image to a 375px phone wastes bandwidth and hurts performance.
lists available image files and their widths.
tells the browser how wide the image will be at each breakpoint. The browser picks the most appropriate source before layout is computed — this is why
when the crop or composition needs to change (not just the resolution). The

For layout logic that requires JavaScript (hiding components, changing data fetching), Angular CDK provides
Prefer pure CSS solutions (media queries, container queries, flexbox, grid) for layout. Use

only when you need JavaScript behaviour changes — like fetching different data or mounting different components. Mixing layout logic into TypeScript increases complexity and risks SSR/hydration mismatches.
Test responsiveness across real conditions, not just resized browser windows:
— Chrome/Firefox device toolbar simulates viewports and touch events. Test common breakpoints: 375px, 768px, 1024px, 1440px.
— DevTools emulation does not reproduce system font sizes, safe area insets (notch), or scrollbar widths. Test on at least one real iOS and Android device.
— inspect component tree and change detection to catch signal/binding issues that appear at specific breakpoints.
— run Lighthouse on the 375px viewport. Touch target sizes (≥44×44px), text size (≥16px body), and viewport meta are checked.
— run Lighthouse on a throttled mobile connection. LCP and CLS scores often differ significantly between mobile and desktop.
— if the user increases their browser font size,

— content overflow is invisible until tested at narrow widths.
includes the browser chrome (address bar) on some mobile browsers. Use

removes content from the accessibility tree. If the same content exists elsewhere for mobile, mark the desktop version
— interactive elements below 44×44px (WCAG 2.5.8) are hard to tap accurately. Use
(desktop-first) in a mobile-heavy project — the cascade compounds overrides; switch to mobile-first

Safe Area Insets (Notch and Home Bar)
Modern mobile devices have notches, dynamic islands, and home indicator bars that can overlap content. CSS environment variables expose the safe area insets:
This requires the viewport meta tag to include

Show different component on mobile vs. desktop
Responsive design starts with the viewport meta tag and a mobile-first CSS strategy: write base styles for small screens and enhance with
for fluid typography and spacing, and CSS Grid's
for fluid column layouts that need no media queries. Container queries (
) let components respond to their container width — the modern replacement for viewport-based breakpoints in reusable components. Serve responsive images with
for JavaScript behaviour that truly varies by screen size; keep layout logic in CSS.
